title: Hormuz Drone Attack Shatters Ceasefire Calm, Oil Near Four-Month Low
subhead: An Iranian drone strike on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz prompted US retaliatory strikes and rattled a fragile ceasefire, even as Brent crude held near pre-war lows. The BoJ raised rates to 1%, the ECB's Lane warned second-round inflation is sticky, and Fed Chair Warsh got the IMF's blessing to scrap forward guidance.

sections.heading: Hormuz Drone Strike Tests Ceasefire as Oil Stays Subdued
sections.body:
An Iranian drone struck a Singapore-flagged cargo ship transiting the Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, June 26, in what President Trump called a 'foolish violation' of the ceasefire reached in the US-Iran memorandum of understanding. US Central Command responded Friday with strikes on Iranian missile and drone facilities, calling it a 'powerful response' to an 'unwarranted' attack. CENTCOM said Iran fired at least four drones at ships in the waterway.

The attack landed at an awkward moment for oil markets. Brent crude settled Wednesday at its lowest level since before the Iran war began — below $73 per barrel, down more than $3 on the day — as stranded tankers continued exiting Hormuz and traders priced in a smoother shipping outlook. Reuters and MSN reported the benchmark had been sliding all week on expectations that the ceasefire deal would restore pre-conflict traffic levels within 30 days.

The drone strike and US retaliation are a reminder that the memorandum of understanding signed June 19 is fragile. Iran has not yet signed a final agreement, and Hezbollah stated this week that Iran will not do so unless Israel withdraws from Lebanon. Trump separately denied the US would invest money in Iran, calling reports of a $300 billion fund 'ridiculous' — contradicting Bloomberg and Reuters reporting that the draft includes a reconstruction fund of at least $300 billion, with over $150 billion already committed by regional partners. The gap between the White House talking points and the draft terms, as an AP analysis noted, is wide.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil markets are pricing a return to normalcy that the facts on the ground do not yet support. The Hormuz drone strike and US retaliation show how quickly the ceasefire can fray, and the unresolved status of a final deal keeps a risk premium under prices even as crude slides.

sections.heading: BoJ Hikes to 1% — Highest Since 1995 — as Yen Pressure Builds
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its policy rate to 1% on June 17, the highest level in over 30 years, in a 7-1 split decision with board member Toichiro Asada dissenting in favor of a hold. This is the first hike since December's move to 0.75%, and it marks the first time rates have hit 1% since 1995.

The tightening comes despite Japan's core inflation sitting at just 1.4% in April — well below the BoJ's 2% target. But the central bank argued that the pass-through from rising crude oil prices is accelerating in business-to-business transactions, and the producer price index rose 6.3% year-on-year in May, its fastest pace in over three years, mainly driven by energy costs.

The yen barely moved on the decision, strengthening only marginally to 160.22 per dollar. Japan spent a reported 11.7 trillion yen ($73.5 billion) on intervention in May alone, only to see the yen drift back toward 160. As J.P. Morgan Asset Management's Tai Hui noted, the overwhelming 7-1 vote signals the board prioritizes inflation concerns over growth. The BoJ also announced it will continue reducing JGB purchases by 200 billion yen per quarter before settling at 2 trillion yen monthly from April 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is burning through foreign reserves to defend a currency that keeps weakening because monetary policy remains accommodative relative to the Fed. The rate hike to 1% is a step toward normalization, but with the yen still at 160, the gap between Japanese and US rates remains enormous. More intervention or further hikes are likely.

sections.heading: ECB's Lane Warns Inflation Stays Sticky Despite Iran Deal
sections.body:
ECB chief economist Philip Lane delivered a blunt message on June 16: even a swift diplomatic resolution of the Iran conflict will not erase the inflationary damage already baked into the eurozone. The ECB hiked its deposit facility rate to 2.25% that same day, a 25-basis-point increase marking its first rate rise in nearly three years.

Revised Eurosystem staff projections now show headline inflation at 3.0% for 2026, 2.3% for 2027, with a return to the 2% target only by 2028. Lane drew a pointed distinction from the 2022 Russia shock, arguing the Iran conflict carries a genuinely global scope — disrupting oil markets, supply chains, and shipping routes in ways the Ukraine crisis did not.

Second-round effects are the real concern: workers demanding higher wages to keep up with rising costs, companies passing those increases on to consumers, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. Lane warned about 'non-linear price dynamics' — meaning inflation could accelerate unpredictably if energy costs stay elevated. The spread between the ECB's 3.0% inflation forecast and its 2% target is the clearest signal of how much further tightening might be needed.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is on a different path from the Fed. While Warsh pulls back on guidance, Lane is making the case for more hikes if inflation does not cool. The 2.25% rate may not be the terminal rate — markets should watch whether the ECB hikes again at the next meeting.

sections.heading: Fed Chair Warsh Ditches Forward Guidance, Gets IMF Endorsement
sections.body:
Fed Chair Kevin Warsh announced at his first FOMC meeting that the Federal Reserve will reduce its forward rate guidance, dropping the detailed path projections that markets had relied on for years. He also convened a task force to review the Fed's communications practices.

IMF chief economist Pierre-Olivier Gourinchas endorsed the shift on June 26, calling it 'entirely appropriate.' Gourinchas argued that overly rigid guidance proved costly when inflation surged, as it tied the Fed's hands. The PCE price index — the Fed's preferred inflation measure — hit a three-year high in May, keeping rate-hike talk in play. Goldman Sachs Asset Management's Lindsay Rosner said there is a 'decent chance' the Fed hikes rates in July.

The Dallas Fed's trimmed mean PCE has held between 2.3% and 2.4% since December 2025, but the six-month annualized rate climbed to 2.5% in May from 2.3% in April. Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson said Warsh still expects a rate hike this year. Warsh skipped the traditional 'dot plot' for his own rate-path view, leaving markets with less clarity about the terminal rate — which appears to be exactly the point.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh is deliberately making the Fed less predictable, betting that opacity gives the central bank more room to react. Markets now have to price in a wider range of outcomes, which increases volatility but also gives the Fed flexibility to respond to an inflation picture that remains uncomfortable.

sections.heading: EU 21st Russia Sanctions Package: Oil Cap Frozen, LNG Targeted for First Time
sections.body:
The EU is finalizing its 21st sanctions package on Russia, and it marks a shift from designation to enforcement. The oil price cap stays frozen at $60 per barrel — blocking a proposed increase to $65 that had been under discussion. The freeze is designed to prevent Moscow from capturing any windfall from elevated global oil prices driven by the Iran conflict.

For the first time, the package includes restrictions on Russian LNG, targeting both re-exports through EU ports to third countries and setting a timeline for winding down existing supply contracts. This is a breakthrough: Belgium, France, and Spain had blocked LNG restrictions for over a year, citing energy security and contractual obligations. That resistance has now broken.

More than 20 additional shadow fleet tankers are being blacklisted, raising the total to well over 300 vessels that can no longer access EU ports, insurance, or maritime services. France seized a shadow fleet tanker off Brittany on June 1 — the most high-profile enforcement action under the framework. Canada's Prime Minister Carney also announced new Russia sanctions at the G7, and the UK seized a Russian vessel in the English Channel.
sections.whyItMatters: The EU is moving from sanction-as-statement to sanction-as-enforcement. The LNG provision is the biggest policy shift — it signals to the market that Russian gas has a definite expiry date in Europe. The oil cap freeze means any Iran-driven oil price spike will not benefit Moscow's budget.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and CXMT as Trade Tensions Simmer
sections.body:
The US Commerce Department has held off adding Chinese AI developer DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese tech firms to its Entity List, despite the designations being approved by an interagency committee. The nearly eight-month gap in Entity List updates is the longest in over a decade, according to Reuters.

The delay is a deliberate choice by the Trump administration to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing during ongoing trade negotiations. At least 75 of the queued firms are connected to advanced semiconductor manufacturing, AI technology, and military supply chains. DeepSeek has been accused of using shell companies to access restricted Nvidia chips. CXMT is China's most advanced domestic DRAM manufacturer and has been designated a Chinese military company by the Pentagon.

China's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian said China 'firmly opposes' the US overstretching national security concepts and suppressing Chinese enterprises. Experts quoted by the Global Times called the hold 'a positive signal to help further stabilize bilateral ties,' while national security critics in Washington argue the freeze creates a window during which US technology can continue flowing to flagged firms.
sections.whyItMatters: The approved-but-unimplemented blacklisting functions as a regulatory overhang over the entire semiconductor supply chain. Any shift in US-China relations could trigger rapid activation of 100+ designations. Companies with Chinese exposure in chips and AI should be stress-testing their supply chains.

sections.heading: Singapore Exports Surge 38% on AI Demand, Gold Holds Above $4,000
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports rose 38.4% year-on-year in May, the sharpest growth in over two decades, driven almost entirely by AI-related electronics demand. Electronics exports surged 94.8%, with integrated circuits up 80.9% and disk media products jumping 227.8%. Exports to Taiwan rose 135.2%, to the US 80.9%, and to China 31%.

Analysts traced the surge to a 'single concentrated force': Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively lifting their 2026 capex plans to more than $700 billion. As eToro's Zavier Wong put it, 'a handful of the world's largest technology companies are all scaling toward this direction simultaneously.' Singapore's factory output has now risen for nine consecutive months.

Gold, meanwhile, posted a fourth straight weekly decline but held above $4,000 per ounce — closing Friday at $4,045.43 — as the dollar weakened on slightly reduced rate-hike expectations following the PCE data. The metal remains in a long-term uptrend despite the weekly losses, with Peter Schiff calling the recent $68 drop a buying signal.
sections.whyItMatters: The AI capex cycle is now the dominant driver of Asian trade flows, offsetting the geopolitical headwinds from the Iran war. Singapore's numbers confirm that hyperscaler spending is translating directly into semiconductor and electronics demand — and that demand is concentrated in a handful of firms whose capex decisions ripple through the entire supply chain.

bottomLine: A fragile ceasefire in the Gulf was rattled by an Iranian drone attack on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz and swift US retaliatory strikes — a reminder that the Iran deal is far from final and oil markets are pricing in normalcy that may not hold. Central banks are diverging: the BoJ is tightening into yen weakness, the ECB is warning of sticky inflation and may hike again, and Fed Chair Warsh is deliberately pulling back on forward guidance with the IMF's endorsement. The EU's 21st Russia sanctions package is its most enforcement-focused yet, freezing the oil cap and targeting LNG for the first time. Meanwhile, the AI capex cycle is supercharging Singapore's exports, with the $700 billion hyperscaler buildout now the single most powerful force in global semiconductor trade.
sources: Reuters, CNBC, Bloomberg, MSN, CryptoBriefing, Global Times, Channel News Asia, USA Today, Seeking Alpha, 247 Wall St, Fortune, Indian Express, Fox News, NPR, Steptoe, euinsider.eu, Singapore Business Times, Straits Times, AP, Gulf states reporting via The Conversation and CNBC
